1: Lĕtā'ăwāh yĕbaqqēš niprad = 'For his desire/appetite the one who separates himself seeks' — the enigmatic opening verse: the person who isolates himself (niprad) does so to pursue his own desire (tā'ăwāh). Bĕkol-tûšîyyāh yitgallā' = 'against all sound wisdom he breaks out' — the self-isolating person is described as acting against communal wisdom; the verse critiques antisocial individualism as desire-driven.
2: Lō' yāḥpoṣ kĕsîl bitbûnāh = 'A fool has no delight in understanding (tĕbûnāh)' — not stupidity but deliberate disinterest. Kî 'im-bĕhitgallôt libbô = 'but only in revealing/exposing his heart' — the fool's goal in speech is self-expression, not learning or communication. The diagnostic: what is your speech for?
3: Bĕbô'-rāšā' bā' gam-bûz = 'When the wicked comes, comes also contempt' — the wicked person brings an atmosphere of contempt with him; wickedness and contempt are package-deal companions. Wĕ'im-qālôn ḥerpāh = 'and with dishonor comes reproach' — a descending sequence: wickedness → contempt → dishonor → reproach.
4: Mayim 'ămuqqîm dibrê pî-'îš = 'Deep waters are the words of a man's mouth' — the depth of speech is the depth of the person; naḥal nōbēa' mĕqôr ḥokmāh = 'a flowing/gushing stream is the fountain of wisdom.' Two water-images in parallel: deep waters suggest the unfathomable resources of the wise person's speech; the flowing brook suggests accessibility and generosity. See also Proverbs 20:5.
5: Śe'ēt pĕnê rāšā' lō'-ṭôb = 'To show partiality/lift the face of the wicked is not good' — nāśā' pānîm (lift the face = show favor/partiality) in legal contexts is the act of letting the guilty off. Lĕhaṭṭôt ṣaddîq bammišpāṭ = 'to thrust aside the righteous in judgment' — the double injustice of the corrupt court echoes Proverbs 17:15.
6: Śiptê ḵĕsîl yābō'û bĕrîb = 'The lips of a fool come into strife/contention' — the fool's speech generates conflict automatically. Ûpîw lĕmahălummôt yiqrā' = 'and his mouth calls for blows' — the fool's mouth invites physical punishment as the natural response to the offense his words generate.
7: Pî-ḵĕsîl mĕḥittāh-lô = 'The fool's mouth is his ruin/destruction' (mĕḥittāh = destruction/terror/ruin) — the auto-destruction of the fool through speech. Wĕśĕpātāyw môqēš napšô = 'and his lips are a trap for his soul' — the lips that set the trap in which the soul is caught; speech as self-entrapment.
8: Dibrê nirgan kĕmitlahamîm = 'The words of a gossip/whisperer are like delicacies' (mithlahamîm = dainties/sweet morsels) — the appetizing quality of gossip: it is consumed greedily. Wĕhēm yārdû ḥadrê-bāṭen = 'and they go down into the inner chambers of the belly' — gossip enters the deep interior of the listener and cannot easily be expelled; once absorbed, it becomes part of the hearer's inner life.
9: Gam mitrappeh bĕmĕla'ktô = 'Also the one who is slack/negligent in his work' — the slothful worker is placed in explicit parallel with the destroyer: 'āḥ hû' lĕba'al mašḥît = 'he is a brother to the one who destroys.' The apparently mild sin of laziness in one's work is categorized as destroying: negligence and active destruction belong in the same moral family.
10: Migdal-'ōz šēm YHWH = 'A strong/mighty tower is the name of the LORD' — migdal 'ōz (tower of strength) is a military fortress metaphor applied to YHWH's name. Bô-yārûṣ ṣaddîq wĕniśgāb = 'the righteous runs into it and is set on high/protected' — śāgab (be set on high, inaccessible to attack) describes the military safety of the elevated fortification. The righteous person's refuge is not a place but a name — YHWH's personal identity and character.
11: Hôn 'āšîr qiryat 'uzzô = 'The wealth of the rich is his strong city (qiryat 'uzzô)' — the rich person treats wealth as a fortress; ûkĕḥômāh niśgābāh bĕmaśkîtô = 'and like a high wall in his imagination/conceit.' The word maśkît (imagination/mental picture) reveals that the wealthy person's fortress is ultimately psychological — the protection exists in how he perceives his wealth, not in its actual power. Contrast with v10's genuine fortress.
12: Lifnê-šeber yigbah lēb-'îš = 'Before destruction the heart of a man is proud' — echoing 16:18 but varying the verb: gābah (be high/proud). Wĕlifnê kābôd 'ănāwāh = 'and before honor is humility' — the reversal pattern of the kingdom: what precedes honor is the opposite of what human nature expects. See Luke 14:11; James 4:10.
13: Mēšîb dābār beṭerem yišmā' = 'The one who gives an answer before he hears' — premature response before adequate listening. 'Iwwelet hî'-lô ûkĕlimmāh = 'it is folly and shame to him' — the fool exposes himself by speaking before understanding; the shame is both cognitive (he's wrong) and social (he's seen to be wrong).
14: Rûaḥ-'îš yĕkalkēl maḥălēhû = 'The spirit of a man sustains/bears his illness' — resilient inner spirit enables endurance of physical suffering; wĕrûaḥ nĕkē'āh mî yiśśā'ennāh = 'but a crushed spirit, who can bear it?' The crushed spirit (nĕkē'āh, stricken/smitten) is the one load the human frame cannot carry; external suffering is more bearable than internal collapse.
15: Lēb nābôn yiqneh-da'at = 'The discerning/understanding heart acquires knowledge' — active pursuit: qānāh (acquire) as in Proverbs 4:5,7. Wĕ'ōzen ḥăkāmîm tĕbaqqēš-da'at = 'and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge' — the complementary image: the discerning heart acquires and the wise ear seeks; both instruments of reception are active.
16: Mattān 'ādām yarḥîb lô = 'A gift of a man makes room for him' (rāḥab = make wide/spacious) — the gift opens doors and creates social access. Wĕlifnê gĕdōlîm yanḥennû = 'and it leads him before great men' — gifts as social currency enabling access to power. The observation is pragmatic, not endorsement of bribery (cf. Proverbs 17:23).
17: Ṣaddîq hārī'šôn bĕrîbô = 'The first to state his case seems righteous' — the rhetorical advantage of being first: before cross-examination, any case appears strong. Ûbā' rē'ēhû ûbĕqārô = 'but his neighbor comes and searches/examines him' — judicial process requires adversarial scrutiny; the truth of any claim is tested by challenge. This is the wisdom basis for adversarial legal systems.
18: Midyānîm yašbît haggôrāl = 'Lots put an end to contentions' — the lot as a conflict-resolution mechanism removes the human element of partisanship; YHWH controls the lot (Proverbs 16:33). Ûbên 'aṣûmîm yiphrîd = 'and it separates between the powerful' — when human negotiation between strong parties fails, divine arbitration through the lot provides a result that both must accept.
19: 'Āḥ nipšā' miqiryat-'āz = 'A brother offended is harder than a strong city' — the most difficult reconciliation is with one you were closest to; the betrayed intimate becomes impenetrable. Ûmdānîm kimbrîaḥ 'armon = 'and contentions are like the bars of a fortress' — the relational barricade that follows betrayal between brothers is structurally as resistant as castle-bars.
20: Mippĕrî pî-'îš tiśba' biṭnô = 'From the fruit of his mouth a man's belly is satisfied' — the harvest-metaphor for speech-consequences: what comes from the mouth returns to nourish or harm the speaker. Tĕbû'at śĕpātāyw yiśbā' = 'the harvest of his lips fills him' — the speaker reaps what he sows with his words.
21: Māwet wĕḥayyîm bĕyad-lāšôn = 'Death and life are in the hand/power (yād) of the tongue' — the most sweeping claim in Proverbs about the power of speech. Yād (hand) is the hand of power/agency; the tongue holds life and death in its power as a hand holds a tool. Wĕ'ōhăbêhā yō'kal piryāh = 'and those who love it will eat its fruit' — the tongue-lover reaps exactly what speech produces: life or death.
22: Māṣā' 'iššāh māṣā' ṭôb = 'Whoever finds a wife finds a good thing' — māṣā' (find) with ṭôb (good thing/goodness) frames marriage as a discovered gift, not a made arrangement. Wayyāpeq rāṣôn mēYHWH = 'and receives favor (rāṣôn) from the LORD' — the good wife is a form of YHWH's favor; marriage is framed as theologically significant, not merely socially convenient.
23: Taḥănûnîm yĕdabbēr-rāš = 'With supplications/entreaties the poor speaks' — the asymmetry of social speech: the poor must beg; wĕ'āšîr ya'aneh 'azzôt = 'but the rich answers harshly/rudely' ('azzôt = harsh/insolent words). Proverbs observes the economic sociology of speech: your social position determines both how you speak and how you are heard.
24: 'Îš rē'îm lĕhitrō'ēa' = 'A man of many companions may come to ruin' (or 'must show himself friendly') — the opening phrase is disputed: lĕhitrō'ēa' = 'to show friendship/associate' or 'to his own ruin.' Wĕyēš 'ōhēb dābēq mē'āḥ = 'but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother' — the quality-vs-quantity contrast: a single faithful friend who dābaq (cleaves/clings) surpasses the shallow social network of many companions.
